refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-005 Refined: The NGHE solution shall automatically derive performance requirements (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability, safety margins) from worksite mission context and operational parameters, record them in a traceable baseline, and maintain a managed history of all requirement updates and calibration changes with full audit trail.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR was vague on 'method' and lacked explicit traceability and history management. Refinement adds specificity: (1) automatic derivation from mission context, (2) explicit baseline recording, (3) managed history with audit trail. This aligns with acceptance criteria demanding documented performance targets traceable to worksite inputs and maintained baselines/calibration updates. High safety criticality demands explicit safety margin derivation and audit capability.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verification shall confirm: (a) performance requirements are automatically computed from worksite mission inputs (e.g., terrain, payload, cycle count); (b) baseline is recorded with timestamp, source, and derivation method; (c) history log captures all updates with reason, timestamp, and approver; (d) traceability chain from worksite input → derived requirement → baseline → history is complete and auditable; (e) safety margins are explicitly calculated and documented.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-005.1: Ingest worksite mission context (terrain, payload, cycle targets, safety constraints) from Site Manager input.
CR-NGHE-005.2: Compute performance requirements (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability, safety margins) using mission-context-driven algorithms.
CR-NGHE-005.3: Record derived performance requirements as a baseline with metadata (timestamp, source, derivation method, approver).
CR-NGHE-005.4: Maintain a managed history of all performance requirement baselines and calibration updates with full audit trail (who, what, when, why).
CR-NGHE-005.5: Provide traceability reporting linking worksite inputs → derived requirements → baselines → history.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Centralized Performance Derivation Engine – A dedicated software module that ingests worksite mission parameters, applies deterministic algorithms (physics-based cycle time estimation, energy models, availability calculations), and outputs a structured performance requirement set. Baseline and history managed in a versioned configuration database with audit logging.

DP-B: Distributed Derivation with Consensus – Performance requirements derived collaboratively across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services modules, each contributing domain-specific estimates (e.g., Vehicle provides mechanical cycle time, Safety Services provides safety margin). Results merged and recorded centrally.

DP-C: Template-Based Requirement Mapping – Pre-defined requirement templates matched against worksite mission context via lookup tables and heuristics. Minimal computation; fast baseline generation. History tracked via configuration management system.

DP-D: Hybrid Derivation with Real-Time Calibration – Initial baseline derived from mission context using DP-A logic, then continuously refined using telemetry feedback from Vehicle and Teleoperation. Baselines updated incrementally with calibration deltas.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A: Centralized Performance Derivation Engine with Versioned Configuration Database and Audit Logging

potential_sources: DP-A requires inputs from worksite mission context (Site Manager, Safety Manager) – this is a functional dependency, not a design coupling. The derivation engine is logically independent of Vehicle and Teleoperation operation.
DP-A outputs performance targets that constrain Vehicle and Teleoperation behavior – this is a one-way information flow (DP-A → downstream), not a bidirectional coupling.
Audit logging and baseline versioning are standard practices; no novel coupling introduced.
Risk: If derivation algorithms are tightly coupled to specific vehicle models or teleoperation modes, maintainability suffers. Mitigation: algorithms parameterized by vehicle class and mission type.
coupling_check.notes: DP-A exhibits low coupling because it is a pure computation and data management layer. It consumes mission context and produces requirement artifacts; it does not depend on real-time vehicle state or teleoperation feedback for baseline derivation. This supports modularity and testability.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Worksite mission context is well-defined and available at system initialization (Site Manager provides terrain, payload, cycle targets, safety constraints).
Assumption: Physics-based algorithms for cycle time, energy efficiency, and availability are sufficiently accurate for the NGHE domain.
Risk: If mission context is incomplete or ambiguous, derived requirements may be invalid. Mitigation: validation rules and user confirmation gates.
Risk: Algorithm tuning and calibration require domain expertise. Mitigation: parameterized models with documented tuning procedures.
Risk: High safety criticality demands that safety margin derivation is conservative and auditable. Mitigation: explicit safety factor tables, documented assumptions, and independent review.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: DP-A is sound. Recommend adding explicit specification of: (1) algorithm inputs and outputs (schema), (2) safety margin calculation method (e.g., factor of safety tables, worst-case analysis), (3) baseline versioning and audit log format, (4) user confirmation workflow for derived requirements before they become active.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution module is the top-level system integration boundary. It owns the Performance Derivation Engine (DP-A), the configuration database, and audit logging infrastructure. It receives mission context inputs from Site Manager and Safety Manager, and outputs performance requirement baselines and history reports to all downstream consumers (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives worksite mission context (terrain, payload, cycle targets, operational policies) from Site Manager and Safety Manager. Tasking carries mission parameters that drive performance requirement derivation.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Outputs derived performance requirement baselines, calibration updates, and history records. Telemetry carries performance targets and audit trail to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services for operational use and compliance verification.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraints and policies from Safety Manager (input); outputs safety margin calculations and requirement audit trail for safety compliance verification (output). Supports high safety criticality requirement.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the system-level integration boundary and the natural owner of cross-cutting concerns like performance requirement derivation, baseline management, and audit logging. The Performance Derivation Engine is a core system service that must be centralized to ensure consistency, traceability, and auditability. Minimal diffusion: only standard interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert) cross the boundary.

Vehicle is a physical/operational module focused on mechanical and control systems. Performance requirement derivation is a system-level concern, not a vehicle-specific function. If contained in Vehicle, the derivation logic would be inaccessible to Teleoperation and Safety Services, creating diffusion and duplication. Not recommended.

Teleoperation is a remote operation and control module. Performance requirement derivation is independent of teleoperation mode or operator input. Containment here would couple requirement management to teleoperation availability, violating separation of concerns. Not recommended.

Safety Services owns safety monitoring and alerting. While safety margin derivation is part of CR-NGHE-005, the broader performance requirement derivation (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability) is not a safety-specific function. Partial containment would fragment the requirement baseline and complicate traceability. Not recommended.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Performance Derivation Engine and associated baseline/history management are system-level services that must be owned by NGHE Solution to ensure: (1) centralized, consistent derivation logic; (2) unified baseline versioning and audit trail; (3) accessibility to all downstream modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services); (4) traceability from worksite inputs to derived requirements to operational use. Containment in NGHE Solution minimizes diffusion and supports the acceptance criteria for documented, traceable performance targets.
containment_risks: Risk: If NGHE Solution becomes a monolithic 'god module,' it may accumulate too many responsibilities. Mitigation: clearly scope NGHE Solution to system-level integration and cross-cutting services; delegate domain-specific logic (e.g., vehicle dynamics, teleoperation control) to Vehicle and Teleoperation modules.

Risk: Performance Derivation Engine may require real-time updates based on telemetry feedback (e.g., calibration deltas from Vehicle). If NGHE Solution is not responsive to telemetry, baselines may become stale. Mitigation: design telemetry feedback loop from Vehicle and Teleoperation back to NGHE Solution for incremental calibration (DP-D variant for future consideration).

Risk: Audit logging and baseline versioning add complexity and storage overhead. Mitigation: implement efficient versioning (delta-based) and archival policies; ensure audit log is queryable and compliant with safety/regulatory requirements.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: IM assignment to NGHE Solution is sound. Recommend: (1) define clear interface contracts for Tasking (mission context schema), Telemetry (performance requirement baseline schema), and SafetyAlert (safety margin format); (2) specify baseline versioning and audit log retention policies; (3) design telemetry feedback loop for incremental calibration; (4) document ownership boundaries between NGHE Solution (system-level derivation) and Vehicle/Teleoperation (domain-specific implementation).

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-A (Centralized Performance Derivation Engine) directly addresses the refined FR: it automatically derives performance requirements from mission context, records baselines with metadata, and maintains a managed history with audit trail. The DP is logically sound, decoupled from vehicle/teleoperation operation, and supports the acceptance criteria for traceability and baseline management. No design coupling issues identified.

diffusion_risks: Low diffusion risk: DP-A is assigned to NGHE Solution, the system-level integration boundary. The Performance Derivation Engine is a centralized service with standard interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert). No duplication or fragmentation across modules.
Containment score for NGHE Solution is 95/100, indicating strong fit. Alternative modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) all score <35, confirming that NGHE Solution is the correct owner.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the natural owner of system-level services like performance requirement derivation and baseline management. The module boundary is clear: NGHE Solution owns the derivation engine and configuration database; Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services consume performance targets via Telemetry and SafetyAlert interfaces. Ownership is unambiguous and supports modularity.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible: (1) unit tests for derivation algorithms (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability calculations); (2) integration tests for baseline recording and history management; (3) traceability tests linking worksite inputs → derived requirements → baselines → history; (4) audit log validation (completeness, accuracy, tamper-resistance); (5) safety margin calculation verification against documented assumptions. All verification activities are standard and implementable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: NGHE Solution owns CR-NGHE-005 and its sub-FRs (CR-NGHE-005.1 through CR-NGHE-005.5). External dependencies are well-defined: Site Manager and Safety Manager provide mission context (Tasking, SafetyAlert inputs); Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services consume performance targets (Telemetry outputs). No ambiguity in responsibility or interface ownership.

recommended_revisions: Refine the baseline versioning and audit log format specification. Define schema for: (a) baseline record (timestamp, source, derivation method, approver, performance targets, safety margins); (b) history entry (baseline ID, change type, delta, reason, timestamp, approver); (c) audit log (operation, actor, timestamp, result).
Specify the user confirmation workflow: after derivation, does Site Manager or Safety Manager review and approve the baseline before it becomes active? This is critical for high safety criticality.

Define the telemetry feedback loop: how do Vehicle and Teleoperation report calibration deltas back to NGHE Solution? Should baselines be updated incrementally (DP-D variant) or remain static until next mission cycle?
Document the algorithm parameterization: how are derivation algorithms tuned for different vehicle classes, mission types, and worksite conditions? Provide tuning procedures and validation criteria.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-005 is not atomic; it comprises five sub-FRs (CR-NGHE-005.1 through CR-NGHE-005.5) that require further decomposition. The refined FR is sound, DP-A is well-selected and decoupled, and IM assignment to NGHE Solution is correct. However, each sub-FR requires its own FR → DP → IM decomposition to ensure complete design coverage. Specifically: (1) CR-NGHE-005.1 (mission context ingestion) requires DP and IM specification; (2) CR-NGHE-005.2 (performance computation) requires algorithm design and module assignment; (3) CR-NGHE-005.3 (baseline recording) requires data model and storage DP; (4) CR-NGHE-005.4 (history management) requires versioning and audit DP; (5) CR-NGHE-005.5 (traceability reporting) requires reporting DP and interface design. Proceeding to next level decomposition is necessary to complete the design.
decision.justification_detail: The current level (CR-NGHE-005) establishes the system-level requirement and high-level solution approach (DP-A, NGHE Solution ownership). The next level must decompose each sub-FR into specific DPs and IMs to ensure: (a) mission context ingestion is properly specified (schema, validation, user interface); (b) derivation algorithms are fully designed (inputs, outputs, safety margin calculation); (c) baseline recording is implemented (data model, storage, metadata); (d) history management is robust (versioning strategy, audit logging, retention); (e) traceability reporting is functional (query interface, output format). Without this decomposition, the design is incomplete and verification is not feasible.
next_level_sub_frs: CR-NGHE-005.1: Ingest worksite mission context (terrain, payload, cycle targets, safety constraints) from Site Manager input.
CR-NGHE-005.2: Compute performance requirements (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability, safety margins) using mission-context-driven algorithms.
CR-NGHE-005.3: Record derived performance requirements as a baseline with metadata (timestamp, source, derivation method, approver).
CR-NGHE-005.4: Maintain a managed history of all performance requirement baselines and calibration updates with full audit trail (who, what, when, why).
CR-NGHE-005.5: Provide traceability reporting linking worksite inputs → derived requirements → baselines → history.

assumptions: Worksite mission context is well-defined and available at system initialization (Site Manager provides terrain, payload, cycle targets, safety constraints).
Physics-based algorithms for cycle time, energy efficiency, and availability are sufficiently accurate for the NGHE domain.
Performance requirement derivation is a system-level service, not a vehicle-specific or teleoperation-specific function.
Baseline versioning and audit logging are required for compliance with acceptance criteria and high safety criticality.
NGHE Solution is the appropriate system-level integration boundary for cross-cutting services like performance requirement management.
open_risks: Algorithm tuning and calibration require domain expertise; insufficient expertise may lead to invalid derived requirements.
If mission context is incomplete or ambiguous, derived requirements may be invalid; validation rules and user confirmation gates are critical.
High safety criticality demands that safety margin derivation is conservative and auditable; inadequate safety margin calculation is a critical risk.
Performance Derivation Engine may require real-time updates based on telemetry feedback; if NGHE Solution is not responsive, baselines may become stale.
Audit logging and baseline versioning add complexity and storage overhead; inefficient implementation may impact system performance.
evidence_needed: Detailed specification of mission context schema (terrain, payload, cycle targets, safety constraints) and validation rules.
Derivation algorithm specifications (cycle time estimation, energy efficiency calculation, availability modeling, safety margin calculation) with documented assumptions and validation criteria.
Baseline data model and metadata schema (timestamp, source, derivation method, approver, performance targets, safety margins).
History and audit log format specification (versioning strategy, change tracking, audit trail).
Traceability reporting specification (query interface, output format, compliance with acceptance criteria).
User confirmation workflow specification (who approves baselines, when, under what conditions).
Telemetry feedback loop specification (how Vehicle and Teleoperation report calibration deltas, how baselines are updated).
Algorithm parameterization and tuning procedures (vehicle class, mission type, worksite conditions).
Safety margin calculation methodology and validation against safety requirements.
